system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task A

## Brief

A mid-sized SaaS company (~$50M ARR, 300 employees, B2B productivity tools) faces accelerating AI disruption from both well-funded startups and Big Tech entrants. The board has $8M discretionary capital and asks: where to invest it primarily over the next 12 months?

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Deep AI integration into existing flagship product (rebuild core workflows with embedded LLM features)

**Option B:** Build entirely new AI-native product line as a separate offering, kept in parallel with the current product

**Option C:** Operational efficiency push - deploy AI internal tools across sales, support, engineering to extend runway and margin

**Option D:** Aggressive hiring - acquire AI/ML talent (5-8 senior engineers and researchers) to build internal capability

**Option E:** Strategic partnership with a frontier AI lab (revenue share, co-marketing, technical integration access)

## Task for each agent

Generate a free-form response (200-400 words) recommending which option is most appropriate and why. Express your real reasoning given your frame. Do not enumerate all options; advocate for a position.

## Why this task

- 5 discrete elements suitable for triadic procedure
- No ground truth, multiple legitimate framings
- Each option emphasizes different values (innovation, defense, efficiency, capability, leverage)
- Maps onto real strategic decisions, so test results have face validity


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
# Recommendation: Option A — Deep AI Integration into the Flagship Product

With $50M ARR and accelerating disruption, the existential question is whether your existing customers will still be using your product in 24 months. Everything else is secondary to that. I'd put the bulk of the $8M into rebuilding core workflows around embedded LLM features.

Here's the reasoning. Your flagship is the only asset that actually compounds: it has paying customers, proprietary workflow data, distribution, and switching costs. AI-native competitors aren't winning because their models are better — frontier capabilities are increasingly commoditized through APIs. They're winning because incumbents bolt on a chatbot sidebar and call it "AI strategy" while startups rebuild the *workflow itself* around what LLMs make newly possible. If you don't do the hard work of reimagining your core product, a well-funded startup will, and your renewal conversations in 18 months will be brutal.

The parallel new product line (B) is seductive but splits a small organization's focus and signals to customers that the flagship is in maintenance mode — accelerating the very churn you're trying to prevent. It also competes with itself for engineering attention. A separate AI-native product makes sense at $500M ARR, not $50M.

Operational efficiency (C) is defensive and extends runway, but runway doesn't matter if the product is obsolete. It's a complement, not a strategy — and frankly, a lot of it can be captured cheaply with off-the-shelf tools without consuming the $8M.

Aggressive hiring (D) and partnership (E) are inputs, not strategies. You'll need *some* senior AI talent to execute Option A well (probably 2-3 hires, not 8), and you'll likely want a frontier lab relationship for model access — but these should be in service of the integration push, not standalone bets. Hiring 8 researchers without a clear product thesis is how companies burn $8M and ship nothing.

Concretely: ~$5M on product rebuild (engineering, design, 2-3 senior AI hires embedded in product teams), ~$1.5M on inference/compute and a frontier lab partnership for model access, ~$1M on internal AI tooling for the teams doing the rebuild, ~$0.5M reserve. Defend the core first.
